Manjul Bhargava, Kiran Kedlaya, and Lenny Ng

Also [ABE] = (BE /BC) [ABC] = 1 ‚àí r, and [ECF] = (EC/BC)(CF /CA)[ABC] = r(1 ‚àí s) (e.g., by the law of sines). Adding this all up yields
The recurrence yields P2 = 2/5, P3 = 3/7, and by a sim- ple induction, one then checks that for general n one has Pn = n/(2n + 1).

Note: Richard Stanley points out the following nonin- ductive argument. Put f (x) = Ik=1 (x + 2k)/(2k + 1); then the coefficient of x' in f (x) is the probability of getting exactly i heads. or r(1 + s) = 1. Similarly s(1 + t) = t(1 + r) = 1.

Let f : [0, ‚àû) ‚Üí [0, ‚àû) be the function given by f (x) = 1/(1 + x); then f(f (f(r))) = r. However, f(x) is strictly decreasing in x, so f (f (x)) is increasing and f (f (f (x))) is decreasing. Thus there is at most one x such that f(f(f (x))) = x; in fact, since the equation f (z) = z has a positive root z = (‚àí1 + ‚àö5)/2, we must have r = s = t = z.
We now compute [ABF] = (AF /AC)[ABC] = z, [ABR] = (BR/BF)[ABF] = z/2, analogously [BCS] = [CAT] = z/2, and [RST] = |[ABC] ‚àí [ABR] ‚àí [BCS] ‚àí [CAT]| = |1‚àí3z/2|= 7-3‚àö5.

A‚àí3 By the quadratic formula, if Pm(x) = 0, then x2 = m ¬± 2‚àö2m + 2, and hence the four roots of Pm are given by S = {¬±‚àöm ¬± ‚àö2}. If Pm factors into two nonconstant polynomials over the integers, then some subset of S consisting of one or two elements form the roots of a polynomial with integer coefficients.
Note: the key relation r(1 + s) = 1 can also be derived by computing using homogeneous coordinates or vec- tors.

First suppose this subset has a single element, say ‚àöm ¬± ‚àö2; this element must be a rational number. Then (‚àöm ¬± ‚àö2)2 = 2 + m ¬± 2‚àö2m is an integer, so m is twice a perfect square, say m = 2n2. But then ‚àöm ¬± ‚àö2 = (n ¬± 1)‚àö2 is only rational if n = ¬±1, i.e., if m = 2.
A‚àí5 Suppose an+1 ‚àí (a + 1)n = 2001. Notice that an+1 + [(a + 1)n ‚àí 1] is a multiple of a; thus a divides 2002 = 2√ó7 √ó11√ó13.

Since 2001 is divisible by 3, we must have a = 1 (mod 3), otherwise one of an+1 and (a + 1)n is a multi- ple of 3 and the other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now an+1 = 1 (mod 3), so we must have (a + 1)n = 1 (mod 3), which forces n to be even, and in particular at least 2.

Next, suppose that the subset contains two_ elements; then we can take it to be one of {‚àöm ¬± ‚àö2}, {‚àö2 ¬± ‚àöm} or {¬±(‚àöm + ‚àö2)}. In all cases, the sum and the product of the elements of the subset must be a rational number. In the first case, this means 2‚àöm ‚àà Q, so m is a perfect square. In the second case, we have 2‚àö2 ‚àà Q, contradiction. In the third case, we have (‚àöm + ‚àö2)2 ‚àà Q, or m + 2 + 2‚àö2m ‚àà Q, which means that m is twice a perfect square.

If a is even, then an+1 ‚àí (a + 1)n = ‚àí(a + 1)n (mod 4). Since n is even, ‚àí(a + 1)n = ‚àí1 (mod 4): Since 2001 = 1 (mod 4), this is impossible. Thus a is odd, and so must divide 1001 = 7 √ó 11 √ó 13. Moreover, an+1 ‚àí (a + 1)n = a (mod 4), s0 a = 1 (mod 4).
We conclude that Pm(x) factors into two nonconstant polynomials over the integers if and only if m is either a square or twice a square.

Of the divisors of 7 √ó 11 √ó 13, those congruent to 1 mod 3 are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus a divides 7 √ó 13. Now a = 1 (mod 4) is only possible if a divides 13.

Note: a more sophisticated interpretation of this argu- ment can be given using Galois theory. Namely, if m is neither a square nor twice a square, then the number fields Q(‚àöm) and Q(‚àö2) are distinct quadratic fields, so their compositum is a number field of degree_4, whose Galois group acts transitively on {¬±‚àöm ¬± ‚àö2}. Thus Pm is irreducible.
